next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

The chamber had no proper weather of its own, but battle made one above it. Dust kept sifting out of the seams in the roof in thin grey veils. The stone under Owen's knees answered distant impacts a half second late, as if the castle were taking blows somewhere else and deciding whether to pass them on. Every time the anchor caught a fresh flare from the fighting overhead, the carved ribs around it lit fox-red and the open pages of the Grinlow Leaves showed more script than they had any right to hold. He had been living for hours inside that ugly bargain: let the school break hard enough, and the deeper work became legible.

He knelt in the founder-cut ring with his right hand laid flat on the anchor stone and his left braced against the floorboards of notation he had scratched over the flags in chalk, soot, and blood. Blood had dried under his fingernails in dark crescents and then opened again. His nose had stopped on its own and started twice more. The front of his shirt was stiff where he had wiped his face without thinking. Every breath brought mineral damp, old lake-cold, and the singed-metal smell that came when the ward edge rubbed against his amendment and failed to tear.

It was not mended. It was only no longer widening as fast.

He kept the phrasing narrow because narrow held. He did not ask the world to heal, or protect, or save. Those were theatrical words and the First Art disliked them for the same reason good tools disliked vague hands. He asked for continuance at the seam. For dimness. For a lower answer where the breach had begun to shine. E...
open_hooks_to_carry: Render the ritual as material labor involving blood, phrasing, surfaces, and concentration rather than generic wizard spectacle.
Let the battle above distort the ritual through stone vibration, smoke, and magical overload.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...her eyes off his face. "Not if he's got any sense."

The girl nodded as if that settled it and kept moving.

When they were alone again, Prue said, much quieter, "You can still turn round."

He could. That was the hideous part. Neville was three corridors away. Martha was somewhere above the kitchen traffic, probably swearing at anyone who tried to take the satchel off her. He could go back into the human emergency with both hands and whatever courage he possessed. He could spend himself there. He could be one more boy in a school full of frightened boys pretending not to be frightened.

And below them the deeper seam was opening on its appointed logic, indifferent to courage, indifferent to Potter, indifferent to every name the castle was currently using to save itself.

He heard himself say, very calmly because panic would only have sounded like a lie, "No. I'm not turning back."

Prue's face did not change at once. Then it did, subtly and much worse than tears would have been. She became more exact.

"Right," she said. "I wanted you to say it straight, that's all."

He should have said something then. He should have said he was sorry, or told her to come with him, or asked her to tell Neville. He should have said anything. Instead he put his foot back on the rung.

Prue did not grab him. That would have been mercy. She moved one step aside so he could pass cleanly if he meant it.

"Owen."

He looked down.

"When you hear them," she said, "don't translate it into architecture."

Then she held the passage and the kitchen light behind her, and he climbed down into the wall.
state_deltas_required: The battle is shaking Hogwarts apart while Owen attempts a First Art working far beyond anything he has previously sustained.
Owen gains enough control over the anchor to discover a combat-ending clause and chooses not to use it.
